meta.title: S213 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes E-Class S213 engine — OM654, M276, M256, M177, OM656. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the OM654 diesel explained. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes E-Class S213 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S213 engine typically costs £1,800–£16,000 supply only, depending on engine — the M274 and M264 four-cylinders sit at £1,800–£4,000, the OM654 diesel at £2,800–£5,000, the M276 V6 at £3,800–£6,500, the M256 inline-six at £4,000–£9,000, the OM656 six-cylinder diesel at £4,500–£7,500, and the M177 V8 at £8,000–£16,000. Fitting adds £520–£1,300 depending on engine complexity.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes E-Class S213 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654.920 diesel (E 220 d/E 300 d) is our top recommendation — it's a modern, clean-sheet design that avoids the OM651's injector seal failure and offers excellent fuel economy. The M256 inline-six petrol is also impressive, though its 48V system adds complexity. For the V8, the M177 is robust but oil leaks are a cost-of-ownership factor.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes E-Class S213?

acceptedAnswer.text: AdBlue system failures on the OM654 diesel, timing chain stretch on the M276 V6 (E 400/450, pre-2020), valve cover oil leaks on the M177 V8 (E 63/S), and 48V EQ Boost system issues on the M256 and M264 engines (post-2020 facelift). The 9G-Tronic transmission is generally reliable but requires regular servicing.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes E-Class S213 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, it's one of the more reliable modern Mercedes generations. The OM654 diesel is well-proven and significantly improved over the OM651. The M276 V6 is robust if the timing chain is maintained. The M177 V8 is reliable but expensive to maintain due to oil leak issues. The newer M256 and M264 with EQ Boost are promising but still too new for definitive long-term reliability data.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes E-Class S213 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is an outright 'avoid', but we'd counsel caution on pre-2020 M274 four-cylinder petrols (E 200/E 300) without documented carbon cleaning history — they can suffer from carbon buildup and HPFP issues. A neglected E 63 without valve cover service history is also a risk unless you have a serious budget for maintenance.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my S213 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: AdBlue warning lights or loss of power (OM654), cold-start rattle (M276 timing chain), oil smell/visible seepage (M177 valve covers), or 48V warning lights (M256/M264 EQ Boost) are the key warning signs. Check engine lights on any variant should be diagnosed immediately.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes E-Class S213 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a well-kept £15,000 S213, a £3,000–£5,000 OM654 recon is 20–33% — well within our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. Even the £8,000–£16,000 M177 V8 recon against a £30,000–£60,000 E 63 is just 25–50% — a viable spend. These are modern, valuable cars; replacement almost always makes financial sense. Only on the cheapest, highest-mileage examples would we advise walking away.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes E-Class S213 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 diesel is proven to exceed 200,000 miles on well-maintained examples — AdBlue system maintenance is the key to longevity. The M276 V6 can reach 180,000+ miles if the timing chain is addressed proactively. The M177 V8 is similarly durable, with oil leaks the main cost. The M256 inline-six and M264 four-cylinder are too new for definitive data, but early signs are positive.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the S213, W213, C238 and A238?

acceptedAnswer.text: All four share the fifth-generation E-Class platform and most engines. The S213 is the 5-door estate (2016–2023, this page), the W213 is the 4-door saloon (2016–2023), the C238 is the 2-door coupé (2017–2023), and the A238 is the 2-door cabriolet (2018–2023). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes E-Class S213 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate under the bonnet and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Tell us your variant (e.g., E 220 d) and approximate registration year (pre- or post-2020 facelift) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes E-Class S213 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E-Class estate — from the frugal OM654 four-cylinder diesel to the thundering M177 V8 in the E 63 S. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the M276-to-M256 engine switch that defined this generation's evolution. Backed by 520+ real UK OM654 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes E-Class S213 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the S213 estate specifically — including the 2020 facelift that brought the M256 inline-six and M264 four-cylinder with EQ Boost.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: OM654: 85% confidence — 520 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries in 2025; well-documented engine. M276: 90% confidence — 390 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries; documented timing chain stretch issues. M177: 90% confidence — 370 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries; well-understood V8. OM656: 75% confidence — newer six-cylinder diesel with emerging pattern data. M256/M264: 70% confidence — pattern-data tier; newer engines with limited long-term data.

overview.intro: The S213 is the estate variant of Mercedes-Benz's fifth-generation E-Class (W213), introduced in 2016 as the successor to the S212 estate. Built on the MRA platform, it brought significant weight savings, advanced semi-autonomous driving tech, and — with the E 63 S — the most powerful estate Mercedes had ever built. A 2020 facelift introduced the new M256 inline-six petrol engine (replacing the M276 V6) and the M264 four-cylinder with EQ Boost, alongside significant interior and technology updates.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz MRA (Modular Rear Architecture) • Predecessor: E-Class S212 (W212 estate) • Successor: E-Class S214 (W214 estate) • Sibling body styles in this generation: W213 saloon, C238 coupé, A238 cabriolet.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The OM654 diesel engine family generated 520 real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], making it the most-requested E-Class engine in our data as these high-mileage workhorses continue to serve fleet and private buyers alike.

items.quote: The 2.9-litre inline-six diesel delivers 330 bhp and 700 Nm of torque — effortless, refined, and the most powerful six-cylinder diesel in the range. Injector and AdBlue system issues are emerging as its only real demands.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want six-cylinder diesel refinement without the V8's fuel bills.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre inline-six with EQ Boost is a masterpiece — smooth, responsive, and with the 48V mild-hybrid system filling the torque gaps. It replaced the M276 V6 and is genuinely better in every way.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the most advanced petrol engine in the range.

items.quote: The 2.0-litre turbo four is a solid engine, but it was superseded by the M264 for good reason — carbon buildup and HPFP issues are known weaknesses on higher-mileage examples.
items.whoItsFor: budget-conscious buyers who accept the older tech.

items.quote: The 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 delivers up to 612 bhp in S form, but the hot-vee layout concentrates heat between the cylinder banks — valve cover oil leaks and coolant system issues are the most common, with full replacement costs reaching £16,000.
items.whoItsFor: AMG buyers with deep pockets and full service history.

items.quote: The 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesel offers genuine economy (50+ mpg) with the reliability of Mercedes' modern common-rail system — and avoids the ageing OM651's injector seal issues entirely.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a modern, efficient estate without the V6 or V8 premium.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 is often overshadowed by the newer M256, but it offers genuine performance (5.2s to 60mph) with well-understood, manageable maintenance costs. Timing chain stretch is the main concern, but it's a known fix.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 performance without the newer engine's premium.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Modern Petrol — EQ Boost adds complexity.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve cover oil leaks, coolant system
rows.ownershipVerdict: AMG Premium — high power, high cost.

economicsRule.title: The S213 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,800–£5,000 OM654 recon against a £12,000–£25,000 S213 sits at 10–40% of the car's value — well within our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. Even the £8,000–£16,000 M177 V8 recon against a £30,000–£60,000 E 63 is just 25–50% — a viable spend on a well-maintained example. These are modern, valuable cars; replacement almost always makes financial sense.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S213 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 220 d (OM654) — cheapest diesel recon, best fuel economy.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S213 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 63 S — V8 replacement and hot-vee maintenance costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S213 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 300 d (OM654) — modern diesel with 194 bhp and excellent economy.

problems.title: OM654 AdBlue System Failures
problems.affectedModels: S213 E 200 d, E 220 d, E 300 d (all OM654.920 variants).
problems.rootCause: The AdBlue (DEF) system uses NOx sensors and a heater that can fail — leading to warning lights, reduced power, and in some cases, the car refusing to start if the AdBlue system is not repaired.
problems.recommendation: On any OM654-powered S213 over 60,000 miles, have the AdBlue system inspected annually — a £500–£1,200 sensor replacement is cheap insurance against a £3,000+ engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare OM654 engine prices for your S213 E 220 d →

problems.title: M276 Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The timing chain tensioner can wear prematurely, allowing the chain to stretch — this leads to rattling on start-up and, in extreme cases, catastrophic engine failure if the chain jumps.
problems.recommendation: If your E 400 or E 450 rattles on cold start, don't delay — a £1,500–£2,500 chain replacement is a fraction of the £5,500+ engine replacement that follows if the chain fails entirely.

cta.label: Compare M276 engine prices for your S213 E 450 →

problems.title: M177 V8 Valve Cover Oil Leaks
problems.affectedModels: S213 E 63, E 63 S (all M177.980 variants).
problems.rootCause: The hot-vee layout concentrates heat between the cylinder banks, accelerating gasket and seal degradation — valve cover oil leaks are the most common complaint, with oil seeping onto exhaust manifolds.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of oil smell or visible seepage around the valve covers, have the gaskets replaced immediately — an £800–£1,500 specialist repair is far cheaper than the £9,000+ replacement that follows if oil loss leads to bearing failure.

cta.label: Compare M177 engine prices for your S213 E 63 S →

problems.title: M256/M264 48V EQ Boost System Failures
problems.affectedModels: S213 E 200, E 300, E 450, E 53 (post-2020, M264/M256 variants with EQ Boost).
problems.rootCause: The 48V integrated starter-generator (ISG) and auxiliary battery are stressed by the system's frequent start-stop and boost operations — failures lead to warning lights, reduced power, and in some cases, complete system shutdown.
problems.recommendation: The 48V system is the defining risk of the M256 and M264 — a £800–£1,500 preventative battery replacement at 60k miles is cheap insurance against a £3,500+ full engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare M256 engine prices for your S213 E 450 →

replacementCosts.h2: S213 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the baseline figures — the S213 estate shares its MRA platform with the W213 saloon, and engine-bay access is consistent across the range.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are S213 estate only (2016–2023). W213 saloon, C238 coupé and A238 cabriolet share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style. The E 300 e and E 350 e are plug-in hybrids; the E 300 de is a diesel plug-in hybrid.
